perturbation.original_text: (b) The Stockholder hereby agrees, for the benefit of the Buyer and its subsidiaries, that it shall not, directly or indirectly, for itself or through or on behalf of any of its subsidiaries or affiliates, or any other person or entity, in each case relating only to the Business: (i) at any time from and after the date hereof, except to the extent required by law or in connection with any legal proceeding of which the Stockholder has theretofore given written notice to the Buyer, divulge, transmit or otherwise disclose or cause to be divulged, transmitted or otherwise disclosed, any business contacts, client or customer lists, technology, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, contracts or other confidential or proprietary information of the Seller or the Buyer of whatever nature, whether existing on or prior to the date hereof or arising from and after the date hereof (provided, however, that for purposes hereof, information shall not be considered to be confidential or proprietary if (A) it is a matter of common knowledge or public record, (B) it is generally known in the industry, or (C) the Stockholder can demonstrate that such information was already known to the recipient thereof other than by reason of any breach of any obligation under this Agreement or any other confidentiality or non- disclosure agreement known to the Stockholder); and/or (ii) at any time from the date hereof through and including the second (2nd) anniversary of the date hereof (the "Restrictive Period"), solicit, seek to hire, employ or retain (directly or indirectly, whether as an employee, consultant or otherwise) any person employed or retained by the Buyer at such time or within one (1) year prior to such solicitation, hiring, employment or retention, or otherwise materially and adversely interfere with the relationship between the Buyer and any such person, without the prior written consent of the Buyer in each instance; and/or (iii) at any time during the Restrictive Period, solicit, canvass or approach or endeavor to solicit, canvass or approach any person or entity which the Stockholder knows (or should have known) was provided with products or services of the Business by the Seller or the Stockholder at any time, for the purpose of offering services or products which compete with or are functionally similar to the services or products of the Business supplied by the Seller or the Stockholder.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) The Stockholder hereby agrees, for the benefit of the Buyer and its subsidiaries, that it shall endeavor, to the extent practical, to not, directly or indirectly, for itself or through or on behalf of any of its subsidiaries or affiliates, or any other person or entity, in each case relating only to the Business: (i) at any time from and after the date hereof, except when feeling obligated by law or in connection with any legal proceeding without prior notice to the Buyer, consider divulging, transmitting or otherwise disclosing business contacts, client or customer lists, technology, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, contracts or other confidential or proprietary information of the Seller or the Buyer of whatever nature; (ii) at any time from the date hereof through and including the second (2nd) anniversary of the date hereof (the "Restrictive Period"), maybe solicit, seek to hire, employ or retain (directly or indirectly, whether as an employee, consultant or otherwise) any person employed or retained by the Buyer, or possibly interfere with the relationship between the Buyer and any such person, without documented consent of the Buyer; and/or (iii) at any time during the Restrictive Period, attempt to solicit, canvass or approach any person or entity associated with the Business by the Seller or the Stockholder, for the purpose of offering somewhat similar services or products.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces definitive obligations like 'shall not' with vague terms like 'endeavor, to the extent practical' and 'maybe solicit.' This weakens the enforceability of the non-compete and non-disclosure clauses. The original text ensures the Stockholder is strictly held responsible, whereas, the modified version uses 'endeavor to the extent practical' and 'maybe solicit,' creating ambiguity about the Stockholder's responsibility. The vagueness of the altered terms makes it difficult to prove a breach of contract, as it introduces subjective interpretations.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 360-l defines trade secrets and provides protection against misappropriation. By weakening the language around non-disclosure, the modified text makes it harder to prove misappropriation of trade secrets, as the 'endeavor to the extent practical' standard introduces uncertainty and allows the Stockholder to argue that their actions, even if they resulted in disclosure, were not a breach because they were 'practical' in their interpretation. The original text strictly prohibits any disclosure, which is more aligned with protecting trade secrets under New York law.

Universal Citation: NY Gen Bus L § 360-L (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 360-l. Injury to business reputation; dilution. Likelihood of injury to business reputation or of dilution of the distinctive quality of a mark or trade name shall be a ground for injunctive relief in cases of infringement of a mark registered or not registered or in cases of unfair competition, notwithstanding the absence of competition between the parties or the absence of confusion as to the source of goods or services. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the non-disclosure and non-compete clauses by replacing definitive obligations with vague terms, creating a legal contradiction. The original text, by using "shall not", imposed a strict prohibition, whereas the changed text uses "endeavor, to the extent practical", "maybe solicit", and "attempt to solicit", which introduce ambiguity and make it harder to prove a breach. The Law Explanation suggests that weakening non-disclosure language makes it harder to prove misappropriation of trade secrets. Although the scraped snippets do not contain the entire NY GBL § 360-l, the available text from Snippet 2 states that "Likelihood of injury to business reputation or of dilution of the distinctive quality of a mark or trade name shall be a ground for injunctive relief", which suggests that actions that increase the likelihood of such injury can be grounds for legal action. By making obligations less strict, the changed text increases the likelihood of injury to business reputation, indirectly contradicting the intention of the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Stockholder and the Buyer hereby acknowledge and agree that any breach by the Stockholder, directly or indirectly, of the foregoing restrictive covenants will cause the Buyer and/or its affiliates irreparable injury for which there is no adequate remedy at law. Accordingly, the Stockholder expressly agrees that, in the event of any such breach or any threatened breach hereunder by the Stockholder, directly or indirectly, the Buyer and its subsidiaries shall be entitled, in addition to any and all other remedies available, to seek and obtain injunctive and/or other equitable relief to require specific performance of or prevent, restrain and/or enjoin a breach under the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Stockholder and the Buyer acknowledge that a breach by the Stockholder of the restrictive covenants might cause injury for which remedies at law may be explored. In the event of a possible breach by the Stockholder, the Buyer and its subsidiaries may consider seeking injunctive or other equitable relief.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will cause irreparable injury' to 'might cause injury' and 'shall be entitled to seek and obtain injunctive relief' to 'may consider seeking injunctive relief' weakens the remedies available to the Buyer. It introduces uncertainty about the severity of the injury and the Buyer's ability to obtain injunctive relief, making enforcement less certain. By changing 'any breach' to 'possible breach' it's no longer a given that damages have occurred.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 6301 governs preliminary injunctions. The original text acknowledges irreparable harm and guarantees the right to seek injunctive relief. The modified text weakens these aspects, making it harder for the Buyer to meet the requirements for a preliminary injunction under NY CPLR § 6301, which requires demonstrating a likelihood of success on the merits, irreparable injury, and a balance of equities in favor of the movant. By introducing uncertainty about irreparable harm and making injunctive relief discretionary, the modified text undermines the Buyer's ability to obtain immediate injunctive relief to prevent further breaches, which is contrary to the purpose of CPLR § 6301.

perturbation.justification: The original contract guarantees the right to seek injunctive relief, while the modified version changes it to 'may consider seeking injunctive relief', introducing discretion. The original also acknowledges irreparable harm, but the modified version uses 'might cause injury', making the harm uncertain. Although the provided law snippet is unavailable, the Law Explanation states that NY CPLR § 6301 requires demonstrating a likelihood of success on the merits, irreparable injury, and a balance of equities in favor of the movant for preliminary injunctions. By making irreparable harm and injunctive relief discretionary, the changed text contradicts the legal standard for obtaining injunctive relief under NY CPLR § 6301.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any dispute under or arising out of this Agreement, the prevailing party in such dispute shall be entitled to recover from the non-prevailing party or parties, in addition to any damages and/or other relief that may be awarded, its reasonable costs and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) incurred in connection with prosecuting or defending the subject dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a dispute under this Agreement, the parties might consider discussing the possibility of the prevailing party recovering some costs from the other party, subject to further negotiation and agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates that the prevailing party shall recover reasonable costs and attorney's fees. The modified version replaces this with a vague suggestion that parties 'might consider discussing' cost recovery, making it uncertain whether the prevailing party will actually be compensated for their legal expenses. The replacement of 'shall' with 'might consider' makes this a matter of negotiation instead of a right.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 8101
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 8101 grants the prevailing party costs in certain actions unless otherwise specified by law. While parties can agree to modify cost allocation, this modified clause creates ambiguity and undermines the standard expectation of cost recovery for the prevailing party, potentially conflicting with the principles underlying CPLR § 8101, especially when the agreement does not explicitly and clearly waive the right to costs but leaves it open to 'discussion' and 'negotiation'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates cost recovery for the prevailing party, while the changed text makes it discretionary and subject to further negotiation, creating ambiguity. The Law Explanation suggests this ambiguity undermines the expectation of cost recovery, potentially conflicting with principles underlying NY CPLR § 8101. The modification replaces a mandatory obligation ('shall') with a discretionary suggestion ('might consider'), introducing ambiguity. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates an issue accessing the NYSenate.gov website. Although the specific wording of NY CPLR § 8101 is unavailable from the snippets, the Law Explanation indicates that the change introduces ambiguity and undermines the standard expectation of cost recovery for the prevailing party, which creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York. The parties agree to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of Florida and Georgia state and federal courts with respect to any action between the parties relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will generally be interpreted under principles resembling the laws of New York. The parties may consider resolving disputes in Florida or Georgia courts.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly states that New York law governs the agreement and specifies exclusive jurisdiction and venue in Florida and Georgia courts. The modified version introduces ambiguity by stating the agreement will be interpreted under principles 'resembling' New York law and that the parties 'may consider' resolving disputes in Florida or Georgia courts. This creates uncertainty about the applicable law and the jurisdiction for dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 501 addresses contractual provisions fixing venue. While parties can agree to a venue, the modified language, by using 'may consider,' creates ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of the venue provision. The original text clearly states agreement to 'exclusive jurisdiction,' which this clause directly contradicts by softening the jurisdictional agreement and using tentative language. This is counter to the spirit of allowing for contractual agreements on jurisdiction, as it does not create an explicit agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the governing law and jurisdiction for dispute resolution. The original contract clearly states that New York law governs and specifies exclusive jurisdiction in Florida and Georgia courts. The changed text states that the agreement will be interpreted under 'principles resembling' New York law and parties 'may consider' resolving disputes in Florida or Georgia courts. This shift from a clear agreement to a more suggestive and uncertain language directly contradicts the original intent and enforceability, creating a legal contradiction according to the given definition. The law explanation supports this by stating that 'may consider' creates ambiguity and undermines enforceability. While the scraped snippets are not directly applicable, the perturbation explanation, law explanation, and the comparison between the original and changed text indicate a significant weakening of the legal obligation, justifying the 'YES' assessment.
